29 samples of mudrocks and marls have been sampled from five stratigraphic sections: Jbel Aouzai section belonging to the Ketama unit (Intrarif sub-domain), and Izzarene, Tabouda, Marticha, Taineste sections from the Mesorif sub-domain. The collected data measured on stratigraphic sections located infive geological maps scaled to 1/50.000, (Zoumi, Dhar souk, Bab Mrouj-Taza, Tafrannt Ouerrha and Taineste). The analyzed middle Jurassic sections of the Intrarif and Mesorif units start with Bathonian and reaches late Oxfordian (Fig. 4).
The Jbel Aouzai section, similarly to other Mesorif sections, starts by Bathonian about 500m black shales and marls bearing-Posidonia laying on liassic limestones and Triassic gypsum with gabbro boulders by mean of extensional fault (Figs. 3 and 4c). The latter is followed by about 300 m of Lower-middle Callovian yellowish-greenish pelites (Figs. 3 and 4c-d). The Tabouda section starts with gray mudrocks and marls bearing Posidonia of the Bathonian age alternating with light gray marly limestones and thin-bedded sandstones.
The Mesorif Callovian succession is thick about xxx m of the sub-domain crops out in Tabouda, Izzarene, and Marticha sections with XXXm, XXXm and XXX m thick respectively. They are represented by puri-hectometer gray and green nodular mudrocks, marls, and rare black shale intervals alternating with sandstones lobes, and by an alternation of clays and marls intervals with medium-grained quartzarenite beds (Figs. 3 and 4 e-f).
The Oxfordian is thick about 300m thick in the Tabouda section and displays varicolored mudrocks and marls (Fig.4. Photo h ) alternating with thin-bedded sandstones and they are topped by gray and green marls overlaid by an alternation of thin-bedded limestones and grayish mudrocks and marls probably of late Oxfrdian-lower Kimmerdgian?. The Marticha section located at the NW of Gaouzat on the road Tainest-Taza is represented by thin and medium-bedded sandstones organized in 10m-15m thick sandstone lobes alternating with hectometer-thick light-colored green nodular marls and mudrocks with intercalation at places of plurimeter-thick varicolored marls and mudrocks, and silts evolving upward to green marls and thin-bedded sandstones. 
At the East of the Tainest Village, the Callovian succession in the Tainest section is about (XXthick) and mainly made of hectometer-thick black shale intervals alternating with decameter-sized lobes of decimeter-thick sandstones and marls alternation, and also thin black shale layers alternating with thin-bedded sandstones (Fig.4. Photo i-j ). 
In the Izzarene section, the late Oxfordian is mainly constituted of nodular thick gray and green mudrocks and marls alternating with decimeter- to meter-thick sandstone beds.

29 samples of mudrocks and marls were collected from five different stratigraphic sections, including the Jbel Aouzai section in the Ketama unit (Intrarif sub-domain) and the Izzarene, Tabouda, Marticha, and Taineste sections from the Mesorif sub-domain. These sections were located and measured on geological maps scaled to 1/50,000, including Zoumi, Dhar souk, Bab Mrouj-Taza, Tafrannt Ouerrha, and Taineste. The Middle Jurassic sections analyzed in the Intrarif and Mesorif units extend from the Bathonian to the late Oxfordian, as shown in Figure 4.

The Jbel Aouzai section and other Mesorif sections begin with black shales and marls bearing Posidonia, with some liassic limestones and Triassic gypsum with gabbro boulders. The former is followed by yellowish-greenish pelites in the Lower-middle Callovian. The Tabouda section, on the other hand, starts with gray mudrocks and marls bearing Posidonia from the Bathonian period and is alternating with light gray marly limestones and thin-bedded sandstones.

The Callovian succession in the Mesorif sub-domain is mainly composed of gray and green nodular mudrocks, marls, and rare black shale intervals alternating with sandstone lobes. In Tabouda, Izzarene, and Marticha sections, the sub-domain crops out in puri-hectometer. In these sections, there are alternating clays and marls with medium-grained quartzarenite beds. The Oxfordian is mainly composed of varicolored mudrocks and marls and thin-bedded sandstones. These sections are topped with gray and green marls. 

The Marticha section, which is located at the NW of Gaouzat on the road Tainest-Taza, is composed of thin and medium-bedded sandstones, organized in 10m-15m thick sandstone lobes alternating with hectometer-thick light-colored green nodular marls and mudrocks with intercalation at places of plurimeter-thick varicolored marls and mudrocks, and silts evolving upward to green marls and thin-bedded sandstones. At the East of the Tainest Village, the Callovian succession in the Tainest section is about XX thick, and it mainly made of hectometer-thick black shale intervals alternating with decameter-sized lobes of decimeter-thick sandstones and marls alternation.

In the Izzarene section, the late Oxfordian is mainly constituted of nodular thick gray and green mudrocks and marls alternating with decimeter- to meter-thick sandstone beds. Overall, these sections provide valuable insights into the geology and stratigraphic history of the study area.

Sure, here's an enhanced version of the text:

The study area was divided into five stratigraphic sections, each of which was sampled for mudrocks and marls. These sections included the Jbel Aouzai section from the Ketama unit in the Intrarif sub-domain, and the Izzarene, Tabouda, Marticha, and Taineste sections from the Mesorif sub-domain. The data was collected from five geological maps with a scale of 1/50.000 including Zoumi, Dhar souk, Bab Mrouj-Taza, Tafrannt Ouerrha, and Taineste. The analysis of the stratigraphic sections revealed that the Intrarif and Mesorif units of the middle Jurassic started with the Bathonian period and extended up to the late Oxfordian, as illustrated in Figure 4.

The Jbel Aouzai section, similar to other Mesorif sections, was composed of black shales and marls bearing Posidonia as well as liassic limestones and Triassic gypsum with gabbro boulders. These units were arranged in an extensional fault (Figs. 3 and 4c). This was followed by yellowish-greenish pelites in the Lower-middle Callovian, extending up to about 300 meters (Figs. 3 and 4c-d). On the other hand, the Tabouda section started with gray mudrocks and marls bearing Posidonia of the Bathonian age, alternating with light gray marly limestones and thin-bedded sandstones.

The Mesorif Callovian succession, which was about xxx meters thick, crops out in Tabouda, Izzarene, and Marticha sections, with thicknesses of XXXm, XXXm, and XXXm, respectively. The succession was represented by puri-hectometer gray and green nodular mudrocks, marls, and rare black shale intervals alternating with sandstones lobes, and by an alternation of clays and marls intervals with medium-grained quartzarenite beds (Figs. 3 and 4 e-f). In the Oxfordian period, the succession was about 300 meters thick in the Tabouda section, displaying varicolored mudrocks and marls (Fig.4. Photo h ) alternating with thin-bedded sandstones, topped by gray and green marls overlaid by an alternation of thin-bedded limestones and grayish mudrocks and marls with possibly late Oxfordian-lower Kimmerdgian lithofacies. 

The Marticha section, which was located at the NW of Gaouzat on the road Tainest-Taza, was composed of sandstones organized in 10m-15m-thick sandstone lobes alternating with hectometer-thick light-colored green nodular marls and mudrocks with intercalated varicolored marls and mudrocks, and silts that evolved upward to green marls and thin-bedded sandstones. At the East of the Tainest Village, the Callovian succession in the Tainest section was about XX thick, composed of hectometer-thick black shale intervals, alternating with decameter-sized lobes of decimeter-thick sandstones and marls alternation, as well as thin black shale layers alternating with thin-bedded sandstones (Fig.4. Photo i-j ).

Finally, the late Oxfordian in the Izzarene section was mainly comprised of thick nodular gray and green mudrocks and marls, alternating with decimeter- to meter-thick sandstone beds. The data collected from these sections provide a valuable insight into the geological processes that shaped the study area, including the stratigraphic history and lithofacies changes that occurred from the early Jurassic to the late Oxfordian.

Similar to other Mesorif sections, the Jbel Aouzai section begins with the Bathonian period, with about 500 meters of black shales and marls bearing Posidonia lying on liassic limestones and Triassic gypsum with gabbro boulders through an extensional fault (Figs. 3 and 4c). This section is then followed by around 300 meters of yellowish-greenish pelites in the Lower-middle Callovian (Figs. 3 and 4c-d). 

On the other hand, the Tabouda section starts with gray mudrocks and marls bearing Posidonia from the Bathonian age alternating with light gray marly limestones and thin-bedded sandstones.
